channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3534144: [meta] Set Drupal 12 platform and browser requirements by Jan 15 2026
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical plan
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3534144 · 5 contributors · 34 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 platform requirements have been finalized to ensure compatibility with modern hosting environments while balancing upgrade feasibility. The requirements follow established policies that select versions based on availability, stability, and support lifecycles. PHP 8.5 will be required following the policy to use the highest stable version available. Database requirements mostly remain conservative, keeping MySQL at 8.0 despite it approaching end of life, because hosts upgrade databases more slowly than other components and the security impact is low. MariaDB increases to 10.11 to follow LTS policies, and PostgreSQL jumps to version 18 released six months before the beta window.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Requirements: Drupal 12 will require PHP 8.5, MySQL 8.0 (no change), MariaDB 10.11 (up from 10.6), PostgreSQL 18 (up from 16), and SQLite 3.45 (no change), with Composer 2.9.3 recommended.

Technical details

This meta issue coordinated the selection of platform requirements for Drupal 12.0.0 by applying policies defined in separate issues. The PHP requirement of 8.5 follows #3406215, which established that new major versions should require the latest stable PHP release available at beta. Database version selection attempted to follow the draft policy in #3363102, though that policy is still being finalized.

For MySQL, the team decided to keep 8.0 as the minimum despite it being EOL before Drupal 12 releases. This reflects that database upgrades are harder for hosts than PHP upgrades, and security risks from database versions are much lower than from PHP versions. Supporting newer versions is more important than requiring them. MariaDB increased to 10.11 to align with their LTS cycle. PostgreSQL moved to version 18 based on the six-month-before-release window. SQLite stayed at 3.45 as no compelling features required a bump.

Composer 2.9.3 is recommended but not strictly required, as enforcement is difficult and Package Manager now uses local Composer when available. Browser requirements remained unchanged per existing policy.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 4, 2025

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This meta issue ran from July 2025 through January 2026, coordinating multiple child issues to finalize requirements before the beta window. The discussion evolved from initially suggesting more aggressive database version bumps to a more conservative approach based on practical hosting constraints. The team recognized that while policies should guide decisions, real-world upgrade difficulty matters. Database requirements generated the most discussion, with the team carefully balancing support lifecycles against adoption barriers. An announcement was drafted collaboratively and published in January 2026. Documentation updates followed across multiple system requirements pages. The Overview page was later marked obsolete to avoid maintaining duplicate information in multiple locations.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3046985: The word "Second" needs translation context
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: c8e4731 · +48/-4 · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3046985 · 17 contributors · 53 comments

Summary

Drupal now adds translation context to ordinal numbers and time units that were previously ambiguous. Words like "Second" can mean either a unit of time or an ordering position. In languages like German, these require completely different translations. Without context, the word "Sekunde" (time unit) might be mistranslated as "zweiter" (ordinal number), or vice versa. Additionally, ordinal numbers are adjectives in many languages and require different forms based on grammatical gender. Layout region labels, date format options, and similar interface text now include context strings so translators can provide accurate translations based on how each word is actually used.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The change adds translation context to all bare occurrences of ordinal numbers in core's user interface strings. Two contexts are used: "PHP date format" for time-related instances of "Second" in date element dropdowns and Views sort options, and "Layout region" for ordinal position labels in layout configuration files.

In PHP code, context is added as the third parameter to the t() function: t('Second', [], ['context' => 'PHP date format']). For YAML layout definition files, a new translation context key is added alongside each region label.

The issue focused on instances where a bare word appears without surrounding context that would clarify its meaning. Strings like "Second item" were already clear and required no changes. Only standalone words like "Second" needed disambiguation.

The change affects layout files in layout_builder.layouts.yml, layout_discovery.layouts.yml, and umami.layouts.yml, the Datelist element class, and the Views Date sort plugin. Context was added to all ordinal numbers (First through Fifth) found in layout regions, not just "Second", because these also require gender-specific translations in many languages.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 9, 2019
  • Committed: March 9, 2026 (6 years later)

Key contributors

  • jhodgdon
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • hexabinaer
  • sanduhrs
  • rpayanm (Agileana)
  • joachim namyslo
  • m-schmitt
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for nearly seven years before resolution. The initial report in 2019 clearly identified the problem for German translation, but determining the correct approach took considerable discussion. Early questions centered on whether YAML layout files could even support translation context, leading to discoveries about how the POTX extraction process works.

The biggest challenge was defining appropriate context strings. Initial suggestions for generic contexts like "ordinal" proved insufficient because adjectives require gender matching in many languages. The solution evolved to use specific contexts describing what each ordinal refers to, such as "Layout region."

The issue was reserved for mentored contribution during DrupalCon Europe 2020. Stefan Auditor created the initial patch in late 2020 that established the pattern used in the final solution. After conversion to a merge request in 2024, the change received final review and was committed in March 2026. A follow-up issue was noted to potentially add automated checks for bare words requiring translation context.

Lessons learned

  • When a translatable string has multiple meanings that translate differently across languages, provide translation context using the context parameter in t(). Words like 'Second' (time unit vs. ordinal number) and ordinal numbers that require gender agreement ('first', 'second', 'third') need context specifying what they refer to (e.g., 'ordinal for footer region' or 'datetime') so translators can choose the grammatically correct translation for each use. (via jhodgdon, joachim namyslo)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572051: hook_icon_pack_alter() is undocumented
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 65b0772 · +45/-0 · 3 files
  • Discussion: #3572051 · 5 contributors · 26 comments

Summary

The hook hook_icon_pack_alter() was previously undocumented, making it difficult for developers to discover or use. This hook allows modules to modify icon pack definitions that are part of Drupal's core Icon API. With proper documentation now in place, developers can more easily customize icon packs provided by core or contributed modules. The documentation includes a practical example showing how to change the label of the navigation icon pack.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Icon API in Drupal core includes an IconPackManager plugin manager that discovers icon pack definitions from YAML files. Like other plugin managers, it supports an alter hook through alterInfo() to allow modifications during discovery. However, hook_icon_pack_alter() was never documented, making it effectively invisible to developers.

The new documentation was added to core/lib/Drupal/Core/Render/theme.api.php alongside other theme and rendering-related hooks. The hook receives an array of icon pack definitions keyed by pack ID and allows modules to modify any aspect of these definitions. The documentation includes a simple example that changes the label of the navigation icon pack.

Discussion during review clarified that theme.api.php was the appropriate location rather than core.api.php, since it contains other theme-related hooks regardless of whether they can only be invoked by modules or themes. A follow-up issue was opened to explore allowing themes to invoke this alter hook as well.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (28 days later)

Key contributors

  • tstoeckler (resc.)
  • kanchanandhini6
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue provided a straightforward first-time contribution opportunity. After initial guidance on where to place the documentation and what existing hooks to model it after, the contributor quickly produced working code. The main iteration came from a mid-review discussion about whether the hook belonged in core.api.php or theme.api.php. After examining precedent with hooks like hook_library_info_build(), the team agreed to move it to theme.api.php as more appropriate for theme-related functionality. A follow-up issue was filed to potentially expand theme support for the hook. The change was committed to all active branches within one month of opening.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568144: Convert editor_filter_xss() to a element.editor service method
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: bcf27f4 · +209/-137 · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3568144 · 6 contributors · 25 comments

Summary

This change moves XSS filtering logic for text editors from a procedural function into a service method. The existing editor_filter_xss() function is deprecated in favor of Element::filterXss() accessed through the element.editor service. This is part of the ongoing effort to eliminate procedural code from Drupal core's .module files and adopt modern object-oriented patterns. The filtering behavior remains identical, but now uses dependency injection instead of static service calls.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: editor_filter_xss() deprecated; use Element::filterXss() from the element.editor service instead.

Technical details

The editor_filter_xss() function was converted to a public method filterXss() on the Drupal\editor\Element service class. The entire filtering logic was moved from editor.module into the service, including checking whether XSS filtering is needed, determining if the text editor is XSS-safe, verifying filter types, and applying the editor_xss_filter alter hook.

The deprecated function now wraps the service method with a deprecation notice. The element.editor service constructor gained a second parameter for ModuleHandlerInterface, enabling the alter hook to be called through dependency injection instead of \Drupal::moduleHandler().

EditorController was updated to accept the element.editor service via constructor injection and call $this->editorElement->filterXss() instead of the procedural function. Both Element and EditorController constructors include backward compatibility fallbacks that trigger deprecation notices when the new parameters are missing, ensuring existing code continues to work during the deprecation period.

The change preserves all existing logic, including the security checks, format switching behavior, and the ability for modules to alter the XSS filter class through hook_editor_xss_filter_alter().

Upgrade

Replace calls to editor_filter_xss() with the service method:

// Before
$filtered = editor_filter_xss($html, $format, $original_format);

// After
$filtered = \Drupal::service('element.editor')->filterXss($html, $format, $original_format);

If you extend EditorController, update your constructor to accept the new parameter:

public function __construct(protected ?Element $editorElement = NULL) {
  parent::__construct($editorElement);
}

See the change record at #3568146 for complete details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 18, 2026
  • First commit: March 1, 2026 (1 month later)
  • Last commit: March 6, 2026 (5 days later)

Key contributors

  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • sivaji_ganesh_jojodae
  • godotislate

Collaboration

This issue was opened as part of the meta issue to eliminate core .module files. After the initial implementation, a question arose about the editor_xss_filter alter hook during review. This led to a discussion in Slack and the creation of a follow-up issue to explore further refactoring of the XSS filter.

The change was committed to main but required a separate backport merge request for 11.x due to cherry-pick conflicts. After the initial commit, a follow-up issue was needed to fix a service call in EditorController. The migration test failures mentioned at the end required updating test expectations but were resolved in subsequent work.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577569: Fix phpstan baseline on main
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d715ee6 · +0/-6 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3577569 · 3 contributors · 12 comments

Summary

This change removes an outdated entry from Drupal's PHPStan baseline file. The baseline tracks known type-related issues that static analysis tools flag but haven't been fixed yet. A previous issue added a return type declaration to a method, which resolved one of these flagged issues. However, the baseline wasn't updated at that time, causing test failures on the main branch. This fix removes the now-obsolete baseline entry so continuous integration tests pass correctly again.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

PHPStan is a static analysis tool that checks PHP code for type safety and other potential issues. Drupal maintains a baseline file that lists known issues that are being tracked but not yet fixed, allowing tests to pass while gradually improving code quality.

Issue #3560357 added a return type declaration to the getCacheTags() method in the Drupal\taxonomy\Plugin\views\argument_default\Tid class. This fixed a type-related issue that PHPStan had been flagging. However, that issue didn't remove the corresponding entry from the baseline file.

When the baseline contains entries for issues that no longer exist, PHPStan fails because the baseline is now inaccurate. This blocks all tests on the main branch. The fix simply removes the six-line baseline entry for the getCacheTags() method's missing return type, since that method now has a proper return type declaration.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 6, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026

Key contributors

  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)

Collaboration

This issue was opened and resolved the same day it was reported. The failure was blocking continuous integration on multiple issues, making it a critical priority. The contributor identified that the baseline drift originated from #3560357, which had added the missing return type but hadn't updated the baseline. The fix was straightforward once the root cause was identified, and it was committed within hours to unblock other work.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3553402: Remove text references to "Drupal 8"
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a0ca9c2 · +32/-30 prod · +6/-12 test · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3553402 · 16 contributors · 44 comments

Summary

Drupal core documentation and comments have been updated to remove outdated references to "Drupal 8" where the version number is no longer necessary. The codebase now uses generic "Drupal" terminology for concepts that apply to all modern versions, making documentation more accurate and less confusing for developers. References to "Drupal 8" remain only where historically specific, such as in migration paths or when explaining legacy decisions. This cleanup improves documentation clarity without changing any functionality.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The issue performed a comprehensive audit of "Drupal 8" references throughout core. Contributors identified over a hundred instances and categorized them into those that should be updated versus those that should remain version-specific. The final changes removed "Drupal 8" from nine locations where the documentation now applies to all current Drupal versions.

Updated files include API documentation in theme.api.php (removing "Drupal 8" from Twig descriptions), core.api.php (cache tags documentation), and several render element classes. The update message for old schema versions now says "Drupal 8 (or later)" and links to current upgrade documentation. Two @todo comments in Checkboxes and Select elements were updated to link to their follow-up issues per documentation standards. One comment in Renderer.php was changed from present to past tense to clarify it describes a historical Drupal 8 development constraint.

Migration-related references were intentionally preserved because they refer specifically to migrating from Drupal 6/7 to Drupal 8. Test fixtures and migration plugin documentation also kept version-specific references where historically accurate.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 20, 2025
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • mdranove
  • mradcliffe (Kosada)
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • chikaramike
  • roxflame
  • ceri_d
  • binssss
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • kanchanandhini6
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • jbodony
  • bwkerenza
  • lostcarpark
  • dcam
  • sujal kshatri
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue had an exceptionally collaborative path spanning DrupalCon Nara 2025, DrupalCamp England, and mentored contribution sessions. The issue was reserved for mentored contribution and attracted 16 contributors, many making their first core contribution.

At DrupalCon Nara, contributors roxflame, ceri_d, and chikaramike (mentored by binssss) conducted the initial comprehensive audit, creating a shared Google Doc to categorize all "Drupal 8" references. They worked with xjm to determine which references should be updated versus preserved. This thorough categorization became the foundation for the final scope.

The merge request required multiple iterations. After the initial changes, reviewers found additional instances that needed updating. Later, the migrate_drupal_ui module removal caused a merge conflict that sujal kshatri resolved. At DrupalCamp England, jbodony and bwkerenza (mentored by lostcarpark) performed another review confirming the remaining "Drupal 8" references were appropriately preserved.

The collaborative approach demonstrated how mentored contribution can effectively tackle documentation cleanup while onboarding new contributors.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3039248: Deprecate views_ui_contextual_links_suppress(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_push(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_pop()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3742821 · +32/-50 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3039248 · 9 contributors · 40 comments

Summary

Three functions originally intended to suppress contextual links during Views preview have been deprecated. These functions have not worked correctly for several years due to changes in how contextual links are rendered. Investigation revealed they never prevented contextual links from appearing in the preview. Since no contributed modules actively use them and they serve no purpose, they are being removed without replacement.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Three views_ui procedural functions (views_ui_contextual_links_suppress(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_push(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_pop()) deprecated with no replacement.

Technical details

The views_ui_contextual_links_suppress(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_push(), and views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_pop() functions were deprecated as part of the effort to remove drupal_static() usage from core. Originally these functions were meant to suppress contextual links of entities within Views preview results.

During review, developers discovered these functions stopped working when contextual links switched from using #contextual_links render elements to #contextual_links_placeholder. The suppression check in ViewsUiHooks::contextualLinksViewAlter() was never called because the placeholder rendering path doesn't invoke the alter hook. Additionally, the push/pop calls wrapped around building the render array, but contextual links are rendered later during prerender, after suppression was already popped.

A search of contributed modules found only one module calling these functions, which has no stable release. The functions now only trigger deprecation warnings and no longer attempt to suppress contextual links. All related code has been removed from ViewUI::renderPreview() and the alter hook.

Upgrade

Remove any calls to views_ui_contextual_links_suppress(), views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_push(), or views_ui_contextual_links_suppress_pop() from your code. There is no replacement, as these functions have not worked correctly for several years and contextual links cannot be suppressed this way.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 11, 2019
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (6 years later)

Key contributors

  • andypost (Skilld)
  • naresh_bavaskar (QED42)
  • hardik_patel_12 (QED42)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • alex_optim (GOLEMS GABB)
  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in 2019 as part of the effort to remove drupal_static() from core. After several early attempts to refactor the functions into a service, longwave investigated in comment #26 and discovered the code hadn't worked since contextual links rendering changed. This shifted the approach from refactoring to deprecating without replacement. The discovery that these functions were non-functional simplified the path forward, allowing the team to deprecate them cleanly rather than maintaining broken code. The issue took seven years from opening to commit, but the final resolution was straightforward once the non-functionality was confirmed.

Lessons learned

  • When deprecating broken functionality that no longer works as intended and has minimal contrib usage, deprecate with no replacement and schedule for removal in the next major version. Document why the code stopped working rather than attempting to fix unmaintained features. (via longwave, nicxvan)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3571094: Remove RegisterStreamWrappersPass
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a4cda85 · +30/-176 prod · +0/-12 infra · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3571094 · 3 contributors · 8 comments

Summary

Drupal's stream wrapper system now uses a more efficient service locator pattern. Previously, a custom compiler pass collected metadata about stream wrappers during container compilation and injected it separately into the stream wrapper manager. This change removes that duplication by having the manager retrieve the metadata directly from the service locator itself, which already contains all the necessary information. This simplifies the codebase and removes unnecessary code without changing how stream wrappers work for site owners or developers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The RegisterStreamWrappersPass compiler pass previously collected all services tagged with stream_wrapper and called addStreamWrapper() on the StreamWrapperManager for each one. This populated an internal $info array with scheme metadata (class, type, service ID). However, a service locator was already being built and passed to the manager.

This change removes the compiler pass entirely and refactors StreamWrapperManager to retrieve metadata directly from the service locator using getProvidedServices() and instantiating wrappers on demand. The #[AutowireLocator] attribute now handles service locator creation automatically, using the scheme tag attribute as the index key.

The public addStreamWrapper() method is removed since it was only called by the compiler pass. The getClass() method now retrieves the class by instantiating the service and calling get_class() on it. The register() method iterates over schemes from the service locator rather than the internal info array.

This reduces code complexity and removes duplication since the service locator already contained all necessary information. PHPStan baseline entries for the removed method were also cleaned up.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • dcam
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue progressed straightforwardly from proposal to commit in about a month. Longwave identified the duplication after the earlier service locator conversion in #3414627 and proposed removing the compiler pass. Dcam reviewed the changes multiple times and confirmed that addStreamWrapper() was public but not in the interface, noting no additional usages existed. Alexpott searched contributed modules to verify no runtime usage of addStreamWrapper() in released code before committing to both main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568124: Deprecate remaining functions in media.module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 69652b9 · +332/-232 prod · +12/-8 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3568124 · 4 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

Drupal continues modernizing the Media module by moving the last two procedural functions from media.module into the MediaHooks class. This completes the transition to object-oriented hook implementations. The deprecated functions were already internal or used only as form callbacks, so most sites will not be affected. This change is part of a broader effort to clean up legacy procedural code across Drupal core.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: _media_get_add_url() and media_filter_format_edit_form_validate() deprecated in media.module, removed in Drupal 12.

Technical details

Two functions remained in media.module: _media_get_add_url() and media_filter_format_edit_form_validate(). Both have been deprecated and their code moved to MediaHooks.

The _media_get_add_url() function was already marked @internal and had no known contrib usage. It has been moved to a protected MediaHooks::addUrl() method and deprecated without a public replacement. The function generates URLs for adding media based on allowed bundles and access permissions.

The media_filter_format_edit_form_validate() function validated that filter configuration is compatible with media embed requirements. It has been moved to the public method MediaHooks::formatEditFormValidate(). Form alter hooks now reference the new method using self::class . ':formatEditFormValidate' instead of the function name string.

Both deprecated functions trigger an E_USER_DEPRECATED error and forward to their new implementations, maintaining backward compatibility. Test classes were updated to use CoversMethod instead of CoversFunction attributes. The deprecation targets Drupal 12.0.0 for removal, which is allowed for internal functions and form callbacks.

Upgrade

If you call _media_get_add_url() directly, there is no public replacement. This function was internal and should not have been used. If you need similar functionality, implement your own logic based on media bundle access.

If you reference media_filter_format_edit_form_validate in custom code, replace it with the new method:

// Before:
$form['#validate'][] = 'media_filter_format_edit_form_validate';

// After:
$form['#validate'][] = \Drupal\media\Hook\MediaHooks::class . ':formatEditFormValidate';

See the change record at #3566774 for full details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 18, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue took about seven weeks from opening to commit, part of ongoing work to move procedural code into object-oriented hooks. The contributor created a change record and marked an earlier one obsolete to consolidate documentation. After discussion, the team decided to deprecate the underscore-prefixed function _media_get_add_url and form callback media_filter_format_edit_form_validate, even though they were internal or callbacks. Committers agreed these could be removed in Drupal 12 rather than waiting for 13. The review process included feedback on return types and implementation details that were addressed through several iterations.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3436295: ParamConverterManager lazy services are broken and should use a service locator
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 919699c · +32/-984 prod · +116/-140 test · 24 files
  • Discussion: #3436295 · 4 contributors · 30 comments

Summary

Drupal was unnecessarily loading several services on every page request, even when they were not needed. Services that convert URL parameters (like node IDs) into full objects were marked as lazy but were actually being instantiated immediately. This change fixes the lazy loading behavior, reducing the number of services constructed on each request and improving overall performance. The fix was blocked for nearly two years by a Symfony bug that has now been resolved.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Services tagged with paramconverter were marked as lazy: true in service definitions to avoid early instantiation, but the lazy proxy mechanism was not working as intended. The ParamConverterManager used a service collector that immediately instantiated all converters when the manager itself was constructed, which happens on every page request.

The solution replaces the service collector pattern with a service locator using Symfony's #[AutowireLocator] attribute. Service locators only instantiate services when they are explicitly requested via get(), providing true lazy loading. The ParamConverterManager constructor now accepts a ServiceCollectionInterface instead of collecting services via addConverter().

A Symfony bug initially prevented this fix from working. The ServiceLocatorTagPass::map() method called ksort() on services, destroying the priority-based ordering required by param converters. This was fixed upstream in Symfony 7.0.9. The change removes proxy class files that are no longer needed and removes the now-unnecessary lazy: true declarations from service definitions. It also removes the addConverter() method from ParamConverterManagerInterface.

New test coverage verifies that converters are only instantiated when actually needed for a specific route parameter conversion.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 26, 2024
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • godotislate
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue took almost two years from opening to commit. The main blocker was a bug in Symfony's service locator implementation that prevented services from being sorted by priority. Longwave identified the upstream issue, opened a bug report with Symfony, and provided a fix that was merged into Symfony 7.0.9. Once the fix was available, the Drupal change could proceed. Godotislate reviewed the code and suggested adding automated tests for lazy instantiation, similar to a previous middleware issue. This feedback improved the final implementation with explicit test coverage for the lazy loading behavior.

Lessons learned

  • When using tagged services that should only be instantiated on demand, declare them as lazy services or use a service locator. If services are loaded via dependency injection during manager construction, lazy flags may be ignored and all services will be instantiated immediately, defeating the purpose of lazy loading and causing unnecessary overhead on every request. (via longwave)
  • When services need to be retrieved by tag with specific ordering (e.g., priority), verify that the service locator or autowiring mechanism preserves that order. Some service locator implementations may sort services alphabetically by key, destroying priority-based ordering established during compilation. (via longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3414184: Test that the PHP versions in .gitlab-ci.yml match PHP EOL versions
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 5caa081 · +1/-0 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3414184 · 6 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

Drupal now includes a test to verify that every PHP version used in continuous integration has a known end-of-life date recorded in the codebase. This helps prevent a situation where Drupal tests run on a PHP version but the system doesn't know when that version will stop receiving security updates. The test catches missing data early, ensuring Drupal can accurately warn site owners when they're running on PHP versions approaching or past their support deadline.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The PhpRequirements class maintains an internal array of PHP end-of-life dates that powers status reports and warnings to site administrators. Previously, when a new PHP version was added to the GitLab CI testing matrix, developers could forget to add the corresponding EOL date to this array.

The new test assertion in PhpRequirementsTest::testPhpEolDates() checks that the currently running PHP version (determined by PHP_MAJOR_VERSION and PHP_MINOR_VERSION) exists as a key in the $phpEolDates static property. This means that whenever CI runs tests on a new PHP version, the test will fail until someone adds that version's EOL date to PhpRequirements.

This change was validated when PHP 8.5 was added to CI. The test initially failed, then passed after the EOL date was added in a related issue. The test serves as an automated safeguard against incomplete EOL tracking.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 12, 2024
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (2 years later)

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for over two years before being completed. The original report in January 2024 identified that PHP EOL dates could become outdated. The implementation came together in early 2026 after related work added PHP 8.4 and 8.5 EOL dates. The test proved its value immediately: it demonstrated a failing state before #3569877 landed, then passed once the PHP 8.5 EOL date was added. This validation cycle confirmed the test works as intended.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3560844: Update starterkit and GenerateThemeTests to handle OOP hooks
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 23ddf6a · +62/-42 prod · +10/-12 test · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3560844 · 3 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

The starterkit theme has been converted from procedural hooks to object-oriented hook implementations. This affects the theme generation system, which uses starterkit as a template when developers create new custom themes. The change aligns the starter theme with Drupal's modern OOP hook architecture. Tests were updated to expect the new file structure with hooks in class files rather than a traditional .theme file.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The starterkit theme's hook_preprocess_image_widget() implementation was migrated from a procedural function in starterkit_theme.theme to an OOP method preprocessImageWidget() in the new StarterkitThemeHooks class. This class lives in src/Hook/StarterkitThemeHooks.php and uses the #[Hook('preprocess_image_widget')] attribute.

The GenerateThemeTest was updated to reflect this structural change. The test previously asserted that generated themes would have a .theme file and no src directory. Now it expects a src/Hook/ directory structure with a hooks class file. Test assertions were updated to check for the hooks class file instead of the .theme file, and to verify the new OOP method signature rather than the procedural function signature.

One assertion that checked src does not exist was removed since generated themes now include the src directory. The theme generation command correctly copies and renames the OOP hook structure when creating new themes from the starterkit.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 1, 2025
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (3 months later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • cmlara (DM13 Security LLC)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in December 2025 as part of the broader effort to convert Drupal core to OOP hooks. The initial challenge was getting the tests to run locally, which cmlara helped resolve by identifying an SQLite version issue that required updating ddev configuration. After a rebase, smustgrave conducted a thorough search to ensure all hooks in starterkit were properly captured and replaced. The conversion was committed in March 2026 to both main and 11.x branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3567483: Update manager crashing admin panel with uncaught exceptions parsing version strings
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: f78790c · +92/-16 prod · +28/-0 test · 8 files
  • Discussion: #3567483 · 10 contributors · 38 comments

Summary

The Update Manager module could crash admin pages when encountering invalid version strings in release data from Drupal.org or contributed modules. This happened when the string "main" appeared in update feeds after Drupal opened its main development branch. Sites running Drupal 8.9 through 10.2 experienced white screens on admin pages because the version parser threw uncaught exceptions. The fix makes the Update Manager resilient to malformed version data by catching exceptions and silently filtering out invalid versions, preventing crashes from external data sources.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The ProjectCoreCompatibility class in the Update module calls Composer\Semver\VersionParser::normalize() and Semver::satisfies() to parse and compare version strings from external release feeds. When Drupal.org's XML feed included "main" as a version string, these methods threw UnexpectedValueException errors that were not caught, crashing any page that invoked update_page_top().

The fix adds try-catch blocks around three points where version parsing can throw exceptions: when normalizing version strings to filter supported versions, when calling Semver::satisfiedBy() to find possible core updates, and when checking compatibility ranges with Semver::satisfies(). Invalid versions are silently filtered out rather than logged, since site administrators cannot fix upstream release data and logging would create persistent noise for unfixable issues.

A test case was added to verify that invalid version strings like "main" in the release list do not cause exceptions. The fix applies to all three locations where the Composer Semver library interacts with externally sourced data, protecting sites from malformed data whether from accidental errors, hacked sources, or man-in-the-middle attacks.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 14, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • cassius
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • jpoesen
  • cmlara (DM13 Security LLC)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • jastraat (CivicActions)

Collaboration

This issue was reported when Drupal.org opened the main development branch and inadvertently included "main" in release feeds, immediately crashing older sites. A server-side fix in #3567494 filtered out the main branch from feeds, but discussion revealed the root cause was uncaught exceptions in core.

Contributors debated whether to log errors for invalid versions, ultimately deciding against it since site owners cannot fix upstream release data and logging would create permanent noise. Testing revealed the issue also affected sites when contributed modules like Suggestion released versions with invalid version constraints. The maintainers recognized this as a security-sensitive issue: external data from any source (malformed, hacked, or intercepted) should never crash a site. The fix took nearly two months from report to commit as the team carefully considered error handling strategies and tested edge cases.

Lessons learned

  • When processing data from external sources (APIs, feeds, third-party servers), validate or catch exceptions before the data reaches version comparison logic. External sources can be compromised, misconfigured, or return malformed data, and uncaught exceptions from parsing libraries can crash the application. Wrap parser calls in try-catch blocks and fail gracefully rather than propagating exceptions to crash user-facing pages. (via cmlara, catch, dww, longwave)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3560117: [meta] Add Navigation to the Standard profile and recipes
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3560117 · 8 contributors · 17 comments

Summary

This meta issue coordinated the work needed to add the Navigation module to Drupal's Standard profile and recipes. Navigation is the modern replacement for the Toolbar module, providing a new administrative interface. Before Navigation could be enabled by default, critical accessibility issues had to be resolved and the module needed to be marked stable. This change paves the way for eventually deprecating Toolbar and removing legacy JavaScript libraries like Underscore and Backbone from Drupal core.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This meta issue tracked the coordination of multiple child issues required to enable the Navigation module in Standard profile and recipes. The main technical work happened in child issue #3575171, which added Navigation to Standard profile and recipes, and #3560118, which replaced Toolbar with Navigation in the Umami demo profile.

The issue was initially postponed on #3391723 until critical accessibility issues were resolved. These included fixing focusable elements in closed sidebars, high contrast mode refinements, and invalid ARIA attributes. Once the accessibility team confirmed all blocking issues were fixed, the work proceeded.

A performance consideration was also addressed in #3576727, which integrated Navigation's performance test into StandardPerformanceTest. The standalone \Drupal\Tests\navigation\FunctionalJavascript\PerformanceTest became redundant after this change.

The meta issue itself contains no code changes; all implementation happened in the child issues.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 26, 2025

Collaboration

This meta issue was opened in late November 2024 and marked critical because it blocks the eventual deprecation of Toolbar module and removal of Underscore and Backbone JavaScript libraries from core. The issue was initially postponed on accessibility fixes from #3391723.

The path forward became clear once several RTBC accessibility issues were committed to Drupal 11.4, including fixes for focusable elements in closed sidebars and high contrast mode. The core team decided to proceed with preparing merge requests while the final accessibility issues were being resolved, since there would be no code conflicts.

The decision to update both the install profile and recipes together kept them synchronized. By the time the child issues were completed, all accessibility blockers identified by the accessibility team had been resolved, allowing the work to proceed smoothly. The issue was fixed once all child issues were merged.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3577279: Fix service call in EditorController xss
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: a68fc4c · +1/-1 · 1 file
  • Discussion: #3577279 · 3 contributors · 13 comments

Summary

A critical bug in the editor module controller was using an incorrect service identifier in its fallback code. The constructor was trying to load a service called editor.element which does not exist, instead of the correct service identifier for the Element class. This would cause errors when the controller was instantiated without the required dependency, breaking the deprecated code path that was meant to maintain backward compatibility during the transition period.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The EditorController constructor accepts an Element service as a dependency. To maintain backward compatibility during deprecation, it includes fallback code that uses \Drupal::service() when the dependency is not provided.

The fallback incorrectly referenced the service as 'editor.element', which does not exist in the service container. The correct service identifier is the fully qualified class name Element::class, which resolves to the proper service registered in the container.

This bug would trigger a service not found exception whenever code instantiated EditorController without passing the $editorElement parameter. The error prevented the deprecated code path from working, breaking backward compatibility for any code not yet updated to use dependency injection properly. The fix changes the string literal to use the class constant, aligning with Drupal's convention of using class names as service identifiers.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 5, 2026
  • Committed: March 6, 2026

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • godotislate
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This critical bug was identified and fixed within 24 hours of being reported. The issue was opened on March 5th and committed on March 6th, demonstrating rapid response to a critical regression. The quick turnaround prevented the incorrect service identifier from causing wider problems in production environments during the deprecation transition period.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575585: Convert remaining themes to OOP
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cc89478 · +518/-386 prod · +510/-394 test · 40 files
  • Discussion: #3575585 · 3 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Drupal themes can now use object-oriented hook implementations instead of procedural functions in .theme files. This update converts all remaining core themes except Claro, Olivero, and the starter kit to the new pattern. Hook implementations now live in dedicated class files with attributes marking which hooks they implement. This modernizes the codebase while maintaining full backward compatibility for existing custom themes.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Themes previously implemented hooks as procedural functions in .theme files (e.g. themename_preprocess_html()). The new approach places these in a src/Hook/ThemeNameHooks.php class with methods decorated by #[Hook] attributes.

The conversion moved hook implementations from .theme files to classes for test themes (test_base_theme, test_subtheme, test_theme_alter, etc.), the Umami demo theme, and test themes for Views UI. Each theme now has a hooks class in the Drupal\themename\Hook namespace.

For stable9, the .theme file retains the procedural functions marked with #[LegacyHook] attributes, while also creating the OOP hooks class. This demonstrates the backward compatibility layer: themes can keep existing .theme files working while optionally adopting the new pattern. The StringTranslationTrait was added where themes used t() function calls, replacing them with $this->t().

The conversion was largely mechanical, performed using rector. Method names in hook classes use camelCase, and double underscores in hook names (common in preprocess hooks with suggestions) convert to StudlyCase (e.g. preprocess_theme_test_preprocess_suggestions__kitten__flamingo becomes preprocessThemeTestPreprocessSuggestionsKittenFlamingo).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (8 days later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

The issue opened on Feb 25, 2026 to convert remaining core themes to object-oriented hook implementations. nicxvan created the initial patch, which converted most test themes and the Umami demo theme. longwave requested backward compatibility for stable9, which nicxvan added to demonstrate BC patterns for themes. The patch committed to both main and 11.x branches on March 5, just over a week after opening. This swift timeline reflects the straightforward nature of the conversion after earlier work established the pattern.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3040302: "Edit summary" button for the text with summary widget has unthemable markup
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6f2da24 · +12/-6 prod · +132/-4 test · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3040302 · 14 contributors · 38 comments

Summary

Text fields with summary display have an "Edit summary" or "Hide summary" button that toggles the summary field visibility. Previously, the markup for this button was hardcoded in JavaScript and could not be customized by themes or modules. This made it difficult to adjust the button's appearance or add accessibility features without patching core. The button markup is now generated through the themeable Drupal.theme system, allowing developers to override it using standard JavaScript theming patterns. This provides flexibility for sites with custom design systems or accessibility requirements.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton() JavaScript function allows themes to override the markup of the Edit/Hide summary button on text with summary fields.

Technical details

The text module's JavaScript previously created the Edit/Hide summary button markup using a hardcoded template literal in text.js. This string included the wrapping span, button element, and classes, but was not wrapped in a Drupal.theme() call.

The change extracts this markup into a new Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton() function that accepts a title parameter (the translated "Hide summary" or "Edit summary" text). The original JavaScript now calls Drupal.theme('textEditSummaryButton', Drupal.t('Hide summary')) instead of building the markup inline.

The default theme function returns the exact same markup as before, maintaining backward compatibility. Themes or modules can now override this function to customize the button markup, add extra classes, modify the structure, or enhance accessibility.

A test module (text_summary_test) demonstrates the override pattern by adding a custom class and visually-hidden prefix text. The functional JavaScript test verifies that overrides work correctly by checking for the presence of the custom class.

Upgrade

To customize the Edit/Hide summary button markup, override the Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton function in your theme or module JavaScript:

Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton = function (title) {
  return `<span class="field-edit-link custom-class">
    (<button type="button" class="link link-edit-summary">${title}</button>)
  </span>`;
};

The title parameter contains the translated "Hide summary" or "Edit summary" text. Your override should return an HTML string containing a button element that the text module can attach event handlers to. Ensure your library depends on core/drupal.text so the override loads after the default implementation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 14, 2019
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (6 years later)

Key contributors

  • rodrigoaguilera
  • sulfikar_s (Zyxware Technologies)
  • ankithashetty (Specbee)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • mdranove
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in March 2019 and received initial patches within days, but progress stalled for several years. Multiple contributors rerolled patches to fix code style issues as Drupal's linting standards evolved. In 2023, a maintainer requested a test and an issue summary update. The key breakthrough came when mdranove created a merge request with a comprehensive test implementation, including a full test module that demonstrated the override pattern. This gave maintainers confidence in the feature's correctness. The change was committed in March 2026, nearly seven years after the initial report, and backported to Drupal 11.x as a new feature.

Lessons learned

  • When JavaScript code generates markup that third-party developers might want to customize, wrap the markup generation in Drupal.theme() functions rather than hard-coding it. This provides a standard extension point that allows themes and modules to override the markup without patching or replacing the entire JavaScript behavior. (via rodrigoaguilera)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568092: Convert editor.module procedural submit, validate and Ajax callbacks to methods
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3fb558d · +296/-195 prod · +0/-6 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3568092 · 4 contributors · 22 comments

Summary

The Editor module moved four procedural form callbacks from editor.module into methods within the EditorHooks class. These callbacks handle configuration, validation, submission, and AJAX behavior for the text editor settings form. The procedural functions remain as thin wrappers that trigger deprecation notices and delegate to the new methods. This change aligns with Drupal's broader effort to eliminate procedural code from .module files in favor of object-oriented patterns.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Four procedural form callbacks in editor.module replaced with methods in EditorHooks class

Technical details

The change relocates four functions that were part of filter_format_form() into the EditorHooks service class: editor_form_filter_admin_format_editor_configure(), editor_form_filter_admin_form_ajax(), editor_form_filter_admin_format_validate(), and editor_form_filter_admin_format_submit(). Each procedural function now triggers a deprecation error and forwards to the corresponding method.

The new methods are: editorFormFilterAdminFormatEditorConfigure(), editorFormFilterAdminFormAjax(), editorFormFilterAdminFormatValidate(), and editorFormFilterAdminFormatSubmit(). All form alter logic in EditorHooks::formFilterFormatFormAlter() was updated to reference these methods using self::class . ':methodName' syntax for callbacks.

References to the old function names in ckeditor5.module and CKEditor5.php were updated with @see tags pointing to the new methods. The conversion also allowed removal of a PHPStan baseline entry for a missing return type. The functions handle editor selection changes, AJAX updates, validation of editor configuration, and saving editor entities when the filter format form is submitted.

Upgrade

The four deprecated procedural functions still work but will be removed in Drupal 12. If your custom code references these functions directly, update to use the EditorHooks service methods instead:

// Before:
editor_form_filter_admin_format_editor_configure($form, $form_state);

// After:
\Drupal::service(\Drupal\editor\Hook\EditorHooks::class)
  ->editorFormFilterAdminFormatEditorConfigure($form, $form_state);

Similarly for editorFormFilterAdminFormAjax(), editorFormFilterAdminFormatValidate(), and editorFormFilterAdminFormatSubmit(). If you reference these functions as form callbacks, use EditorHooks::class . ':methodName' format.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 17, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • dcam
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue opened in January 2026 as part of the meta-issue to eliminate core .module files. The initial patch had clean automated test results. Review by dcam caught two documentation references in the CKEditor5 module that still pointed to the old function names. Claudiu Cristea quickly updated these with @see tags pointing to the new methods.

After initial approval, longwave identified a conflict when applying to the 11.x branch due to other deprecation work. This required splitting the work into two merge requests: one for 11.x that deprecates the functions, and one for main that removes them entirely. The author created both MRs and both were committed the same day in March after the reroll.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570849: Deprecate SessionManager::delete()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 29b81b3 · +186/-68 prod · +114/-0 test · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3570849 · 4 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

Session management now uses a dedicated repository service instead of having SessionManager directly access the database. This makes it easier for modules to replace session storage backends without overriding the entire session manager. The new approach also removes unnecessary checks that prevented deleting sessions in certain contexts, since the repository can delete any user's sessions regardless of the current user context.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: SessionManager::delete() and SessionManager::setWriteSafeHandler() are deprecated; use UserSessionRepositoryInterface::deleteAll() instead.
  • New API: New UserSessionRepositoryInterface service provides deleteAll() method for managing user sessions.

Technical details

The SessionManager::delete() method previously performed direct database queries to remove user sessions. This was the last remaining database operation in SessionManager, making it difficult for modules to swap out session storage backends without extending the entire class.

The responsibility for deleting user sessions now belongs to the new UserSessionRepository service implementing UserSessionRepositoryInterface. The repository's deleteAll() method accepts a user ID and removes all session records for that user.

The old SessionManager::delete() guarded deletions with WriteSafeSessionHandler checks and CLI detection. These guards are removed in the new repository because deleteAll() can target any user's sessions, not just the current session. For example, when blocking a user, Drupal deletes all their sessions regardless of who is currently authenticated.

The SessionManager constructor signature changed: the database connection parameter is removed and the session handler parameter moved to match the parent class order. A new UserSessionRepositoryInterface parameter was added. Backward compatibility detects the old parameter order and triggers a deprecation error.

Core code in User::postSave() now injects and uses UserSessionRepositoryInterface instead of calling SessionManager::delete().

Upgrade

If your code calls SessionManager::delete(), inject UserSessionRepositoryInterface instead and call deleteAll():

// Before:
$session_manager = \Drupal::service('session_manager');
$session_manager->delete($uid);

// After:
$session_repository = \Drupal::service(UserSessionRepositoryInterface::class);
$session_repository->deleteAll($uid);

If you extend SessionManager and override the constructor, update your parameter list to match the new signature with the session handler as the second parameter and UserSessionRepositoryInterface as the last parameter.

The SessionManager::setWriteSafeHandler() method is also deprecated with no replacement needed.

To provide a custom session storage backend, implement UserSessionRepositoryInterface and tag your service with backend_overridable to override the default implementation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 31, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • znerol
  • dcam
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue revisited an idea first attempted 10 years earlier in #2472535, which had a different approach. The author recognized this connection during the current work.

The initial merge request needed a change record URL update. During review, dcam suggested also deprecating the connection property and constructor argument, which led to reorganizing the constructor parameters to match the parent class. This made the change cleaner but required backward compatibility logic to detect the old parameter order.

longwave identified that the change could land in 11.4 and be removed in 12.0, noting that very few contrib modules extend SessionManager. The timeline from opening to commit was just over one month, with a follow-up issue created immediately after commit to track the removal work for Drupal 12.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576995: Update CKEditor 5 to 47.6.0
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: be296a7 · +420/-420 · 400 files
  • Discussion: #3576995 · 2 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

CKEditor 5 has been updated from version 47.5.x to 47.6.0. This update includes bug fixes, performance improvements, and other enhancements from the upstream CKEditor 5 project. The update ensures Drupal's rich text editor stays current with the latest stable release, providing users with improved editing capabilities and continued compatibility with modern browsers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This is a routine vendor library update that brings CKEditor 5 to version 47.6.0. The diff shows changes across all CKEditor 5 JavaScript asset files in core/assets/vendor/ckeditor5/. The primary visible changes are copyright year updates from 2025 to 2026 throughout the codebase, indicating this is a maintenance release prepared early in 2026.

The update follows Drupal's standard practice of tracking CKEditor 5 releases closely to provide users with the latest editor features and fixes. All changes are contained within the vendored JavaScript files, with no modifications to Drupal's integration layer. The minified nature of the assets means the detailed changes are not easily readable from the diff, but this is expected for vendor library updates.

The update was committed to multiple branches (11.x, 11.3.x, 10.6.x, and main), ensuring consistency across supported Drupal versions. This approach maintains feature parity and security posture across the release branches that include CKEditor 5.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 4, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
